messages.content: re-write the following in active voice: Once a draft of the updated policy has been established, it will circulate for review and feedback through a formal review process in which all relevant commands and stakeholders can provide input on the proposed changes. Incorporating any necessary changes based on the feedback received, the policy's final draft is approved through the chain of command, with higher levels of leadership within the Navy reviewing and approving the policy changes. Once approved, the revised policy will be disseminated to all commands within the Navy for implementation.

Once we establish a draft of the updated policy, it will undergo a formal review process for review and feedback, allowing all relevant commands and stakeholders to provide input on the proposed changes. After incorporating any necessary changes based on the received feedback, the chain of command will approve the policy's final draft, with higher levels of Navy leadership reviewing and approving the changes. Once approved, we will disseminate the revised policy to all commands within the Navy for implementation.

re-write the following for clarity: The Navy released NAVADMIN 022/24 (2024) stating sexual harassment can is now filed as confidential, but OPNAVINST 5354.1H was not revised, leaving the interpretation and implementation to the triad and command representatives. These factors leave the potential for training, educational, and implementation gaps where rumors can create an environment of mistrust or be disruptive to policy changes.

The Navy announced in NAVADMIN 022/24 (2024) that sexual harassment complaints can now be filed confidentially. However, OPNAVINST 5354.1H has not been updated to reflect this change. This lack of revision could lead to inconsistencies in how the policy is interpreted and applied by the triad and command representatives. Such inconsistencies may result in gaps in training, education, and implementation, potentially fostering an environment where rumors create mistrust or disruption regarding the policy changes.
rewrite that in active voice

In NAVADMIN 022/24 (2024), the Navy announced a new policy allowing confidential filing of sexual harassment complaints. However, it has not updated OPNAVINST 5354.1H to align with this change. This omission could cause the triad and command representatives to interpret and apply the policy inconsistently. Consequently, these inconsistencies might lead to gaps in training, education, and policy implementation, promoting an environment where rumors can undermine trust or disrupt the policy changes.

It seems like you forgot to provide the text you want rewritten in the active voice. Please provide the specific text or sentence, and I'll be happy to help you rewrite it!

The working group ensures that the actual experiences and needs inform policy changes for those affected. The group will meet regularly to discuss and draft policy revisions, considering the latest best practices in communication and technology. Once a draft of the revised policy has been developed, it will circulate for review and feedback through a formal review process in which all relevant commands and stakeholders can provide input on the proposed changes. This feedback is crucial in ensuring that the policy is comprehensive, practical, and reflective of the needs of the Navy. Based on the feedback received, the policy's final draft is approved after incorporating any necessary changes. It is through the chain of command, with higher levels of leadership within the Navy reviewing and approving the policy changes. Once approved, the revised policy will be disseminated to all commands within the Navy for implementation. The service must provide for instantaneous multiple distribution of orders and information to the various forces over vast areas and regardless of disposition; direction finding; radio tracking; tactical and strategical communications; communication through deliberate interference; creation of interference; speed; accuracy and secrecy of communications; communications on the surface, in the air, and submerged; interception of enemy traffic; interception of own traffic; and communication of spots from aircraft spotting for firing ships (Hooper, 2019). It is essential to understand the background of Navy leadership communication to continue discussing how we got here today.

The working group actively uses actual experiences and needs to inform policy changes for those affected. They will hold regular meetings to discuss and draft policy revisions, taking into account the latest best practices in communication and technology. Once they develop a draft of the revised policy, they will initiate a formal review process, inviting all relevant commands and stakeholders to provide input on the proposed changes. This input is vital to ensuring that the policy is comprehensive, practical, and meets the Navy's needs. After gathering feedback, the working group will finalize the draft policy by making any necessary changes. This final draft then proceeds through the chain of command, with higher Navy leadership levels reviewing and approving the policy changes. After approval, the Navy will disseminate the revised policy to all commands for implementation. The service must ensure instantaneous multiple distributions of orders and information across vast areas to its forces, regardless of their disposition. This entails direction finding, radio tracking, tactical and strategical communications, communication through deliberate interference, creation of interference, and ensuring speed, accuracy, and secrecy of communications across surface, air, and submerged operations. It also includes intercepting enemy traffic, securing own traffic, and communicating spots from aircraft spotting for firing ships. Understanding the evolution of Navy leadership communication is crucial to our discussion of how we arrived at our current state today (Hooper, 2019).

rewrite the following in active voice: The Navy released NAVADMIN 022/24 (2024) stating sexual harassment can now be filed as confidential but OPNAVINST 5354.1H was not revised leaving the interpretation and implementation to the triad and command representatives. NAVADMIN 022/24 mentions there will be town halls scheduled, but a month into the release of the NAVADMIN, not one Navy website has listed a town hall schedule. These factors leave the potential for training, educational, and implementation gaps where rumors can create an environment of mistrust or be disruptive to policy changes. Internal to the organization, a breakdown in unit-level communications can be just as disastrous but to the detriment of the sailors' morale, the command's climate, the retention rate, and the failure of policies to be implemented or updated.

The Navy issued NAVADMIN 022/24 (2024), declaring that personnel can now file sexual harassment complaints confidentially, yet it did not update OPNAVINST 5354.1H, leaving the triad and command representatives to interpret and implement it. NAVADMIN 022/24 also states the Navy will schedule town halls, but a month after releasing the NAVADMIN, no Navy website has posted any town hall schedule. These issues may lead to gaps in training, education, and implementation, where rumors could foster mistrust or disrupt policy changes. From within the organization, a breakdown in communication at the unit level could prove disastrous, negatively affecting sailors' morale, the command's climate, retention rates, and the successful implementation or updating of policies.

rewrite in active voice: The following recommendations are proposed to enhance fleet-wide policy communication. Firstly, a new program infused with the power of artificial intelligence will be established that consolidates the Navy's policies and guidance. This initiative aims to provide access to any policy or guidance that is prudent to the individual sailor based on their current job, rating, and warfare area. The program would also account for a sailor's credentials and security clearance, and each sailor's "need to know" would be determined based on their position. The AI would then be able to determine what policies are accessible by everyone, eliminating the need for various websites that are separating policies based on a variety of factors.

We propose the following recommendations to enhance policy communication across the fleet. First, we will establish a new program that leverages artificial intelligence to consolidate all Navy policies and guidance. This program aims to make any relevant policy or guidance accessible to individual sailors, tailored to their current job, rating, and warfare area. It will consider a sailor's credentials and security clearance, determining their "need to know" based on their position. The AI will identify which policies are accessible to everyone, eliminating the necessity for multiple websites that currently segregate policies on various factors.

rewite in active voice: Secondly, a feedback mechanism should be integrated into the program that allows sailors to provide suggestions on how a policy may or may not be working and what can be done to fix it. Unlike the Interactive Customer Evaluation system in place throughout various installations that may provide feedback (Commander, Navy Region Southwest, n.d.). This feedback mechanism will be powered by Artificial Intelligence that is already integrated and ensures that legitimate suggestions that are not redundant or malicious are routed to the program advisory team of policy management for review. The status of this review would be updated in real-time and be accessible by the sailor who initiated it. This ensures that any sailor with an idea for an improvement can feel that their voices are heard.

Secondly, we should incorporate a feedback mechanism into the program that enables sailors to suggest how a policy might be functioning or failing and propose solutions. This mechanism will differ from the Interactive Customer Evaluation system currently used at various installations, which collects feedback (Commander, Navy Region Southwest, n.d.). Powered by Artificial Intelligence already integrated into the system, this feedback tool will ensure that only legitimate, non-redundant, and non-malicious suggestions reach the policy management advisory team for review. The system will update the review status in real-time and make it accessible to the sailor who initiated the feedback. This approach guarantees that any sailor with a proposal for improvement can feel that their input is valued and taken seriously.

use this website https://news.usni.org/2021/07/12/lawmakers-survey-94-of-sailors-say-damaging-operational-failures-related-to-navy-culture-leadership-problems to write a essay in active voice on navy communication problems

In July 2021, a revealing survey conducted among sailors highlighted significant concerns about the Navy's internal culture and leadership issues, underscoring the profound impact these areas have on operational effectiveness. Conducted by a bipartisan group of lawmakers, the survey found that a staggering 94 percent of responding sailors believe that damaging operational failures within the Navy stem from problems related to its culture and leadership (USNI News, 2021). This paper examines the critical communication problems within the Navy, as illuminated by the survey's findings, and explores potential pathways to ameliorate these systemic issues.

The survey's results shine a light on the pervasive sentiment among sailors that the Navy's leadership and cultural shortcomings are directly influencing operational failures. Such a substantial consensus suggests that these are not isolated incidents but rather indicative of a widespread and ingrained problem. Sailors expressed concerns over a culture that prioritizes operational demands over safety and maintenance, with one respondent noting, "We are so operationally driven that safety, maintenance, and training take a backseat" (USNI News, 2021). This statement encapsulates the heart of the communication problem: a top-down emphasis on operational output at the expense of critical foundational elements such as safety protocols and proper training.

Furthermore, the survey underscores a pressing need for open and effective communication channels within the Navy. Sailors voiced frustration over a hierarchical culture that stifles dissent and discourages the open expression of concerns. A culture of silence and fear of retribution for speaking out against unsafe practices or highlighting areas in dire need of improvement exacerbates the issue, preventing meaningful dialogue and hindering the flow of essential information to higher-ups who could instigate change. When the flow of communication is obstructed in this manner, it not only affects morale but also jeopardizes operational integrity and safety.

Addressing these communication challenges requires a multifaceted approach. First and foremost, it is imperative to foster a culture of transparency and openness, where sailors feel empowered and safe to voice their concerns and suggestions without fear of negative repercussions. Leadership at all levels must actively encourage and model this open communication, demonstrating a genuine commitment to listening and, more importantly, responding to the feedback and concerns of their subordinates.

Additionally, the Navy must reassess its priorities, ensuring that operational demands do not consistently overshadow essential safety, maintenance, and training requirements. By openly acknowledging the importance of these foundational elements and incorporating them into the core operational planning and execution processes, the Navy can begin to restore trust and confidence among its ranks.

The 2021 survey findings present a stark reminder of the critical importance of effective communication within the Navy. By prioritizing open dialogue, encouraging a culture of transparency, and realigning operational priorities to include a focus on safety and maintenance, the Navy can address these systemic communication problems, ultimately leading to enhanced operational effectiveness and a more motivated and engaged force.

Reference:
- LaGrone, S. (2021). Lawmakers: Survey of 94% of Sailors Say Damaging Operational Failures Related to Navy Culture, Leadership Problems. USNI News. Retrieved from https://news.usni.org/2021/07/12/lawmakers-survey-94-of-sailors-say-damaging-operational-failures-related-to-navy-culture-leadership-problems
reframe the discussion focused more on failures in policy distrobution and clairity

In July 2021, a survey conducted by lawmakers revealed profound concerns among sailors regarding the Navy's policy distribution and clarity, elucidating their impact on operational effectiveness. The survey, which captured the perspectives of a bipartisan group of lawmakers, discovered that a significant 94 percent of participants attribute detrimental operational failures to issues stemming from Navy policies and their dissemination (USNI News, 2021). This essay delves into the core communication challenges highlighted by the survey, focusing on failures in policy distribution and clarity, and proposes potential strategies for addressing these critical issues.

The findings from the survey underscore a critical gap in the Navy's approach to policy distribution and the clarity of these policies. Sailors reported experiencing frequent confusion and misunderstandings due to poorly communicated policies, which are often disseminated through convoluted channels. One sailor’s feedback highlights the crux of the issue: "Policies are often buried in pages of documentation, or worse, communicated through word-of-mouth, leading to misinterpretation and inconsistency in implementation” (USNI News, 2021). This statement underscores a widespread problem: vital operational policies are not reaching the intended recipients in a clear and understandable manner, which can lead to inconsistent application and, ultimately, operational missteps.

Moreover, the survey brings to light a pressing need for a more streamlined and transparent policy communication process within the Navy. The current state, where policies are either not adequately disseminated or communicated in a manner that leaves room for ambiguity, creates an environment ripe for operational errors. The barrier to effective communication isn’t just related to the medium of distribution, but also the content’s clarity, necessitating a renovation of both how policies are shared and how they are written.

To tackle the challenges outlined by the survey, a comprehensive strategy focused on enhancing policy distribution and clarity is essential. First, implementing a centralized digital platform that serves as the authoritative source for all operational policies could significantly improve accessibility. This platform would ensure that policies are not only easy to find but also kept up-to-date, effectively eliminating the reliance on unreliable word-of-mouth dissemination.

In parallel, the Navy must invest in ensuring the clarity of its policies. This involves a rigorous review process where policies are written in clear, concise language easily understandable by sailors at all levels. Additionally, incorporating visual aids and summaries for more technical or complex policies could further enhance comprehension.

The 2021 survey of sailors has cast a spotlight on the significant impact that failures in policy distribution and clarity have on operational effectiveness within the Navy. By adopting a digital-first approach to policy dissemination and prioritizing the readability of policy documents, the Navy can begin to bridge the communication gap. Such measures are not just about improving operational efficiency; they are about fostering a culture of transparency and accountability, where every sailor has the information they need to perform their duties to the best of their abilities.

Reference: - LaGrone, S. (2021). Lawmakers: Survey of 94% of Sailors Say Damaging Operational Failures Related to Navy Culture, Leadership Problems. USNI News. Retrieved from https://news.usni.org/2021/07/12/lawmakers-survey-94-of-sailors-say-damaging-operational-failures-related-to-navy-culture-leadership-problems
